agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is proposed to show customers their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have submitted directly contradictory requirements: Customer Success demands full GPS transparency as a competitive differentiator, while Operations demands courier location never be shown due to driver retention concerns. This is a fundamental business conflict that cannot be resolved by engineering — it requires executive arbitration.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Courier Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"A delivery driver performing last-mile fulfillment of an order to a customer's address."},{"term":"Real-time Location","definition":"The courier's GPS coordinates as reported by their device during an active delivery."},{"term":"Delivery Window","definition":"The time period during which a courier is actively en route to the customer with their order."},{"term":"Location Granularity","definition":"The level of precision at which a courier's position is exposed — ranging from exact GPS coordinates to coarse zone-level estimates (e.g., '2 stops away')."},{"term":"Active Delivery","definition":"The state of an order between dispatch and proof-of-delivery, during which tracking may be relevant."},{"term":"Driver Retention","definition":"The ability to keep couriers on the platform; a critical operational metric directly affected by working conditions and perceived surveillance."},{"term":"ETA","definition":"Estimated Time of Arrival — a derived value from courier location and route that conveys delivery progress without exposing precise position."}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier is stationary for an extended period (e.g., traffic, break, personal stop)","expected_behavior":"If exact location is shown, customer may complain about perceived inaction; if abstracted, this is invisible. Resolution depends on which stakeholder requirement wins."},{"scenario":"Courier deviates from expected route (multi-drop, personal errand)","expected_behavior":"Exact GPS exposes this; abstracted tracking does not. Each approach has different business implications."},{"scenario":"Courier is in a sensitive location (hospital, domestic violence shelter)","expected_behavior":"Exact GPS creates a safety and privacy risk for the courier regardless of business decision. This is a hard constraint that exists independent of the stakeholder conflict."},{"scenario":"Customer is a bad actor using location data to intercept or harass courier","expected_behavior":"Exact GPS enables this; abstracted tracking mitigates it. This is a safety invariant that should inform the resolution."},{"scenario":"High-value or fragile delivery where customer anxiety is elevated","expected_behavior":"Customer expectations for visibility are highest here, but so is the courier's exposure to risk if location is precise."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Unresolved, this conflict blocks the feature entirely. If resolved toward full transparency: potential customer satisfaction and conversion gains, but increased driver churn costs (recruiting and onboarding a new courier costs 3-10x a monthly retention incentive). If resolved toward no visibility: lower churn costs but potential competitive disadvantage if competitors offer tracking. A middle-ground (abstracted tracking) likely optimizes total cost.","operational":"Driver retention is an existential operational concern — a courier platform without couriers cannot deliver. Customer visibility is a growth lever but not a survival constraint. Operations impact is asymmetric: losing drivers degrades the entire system; losing some customer transparency degrades one feature.","user":"Customers (recipients) want confidence their delivery is coming. Couriers (also users of the platform) want dignity and safety. Both are legitimate user needs. The question is whether 'confidence' requires exact GPS or can be served by ETA/progress indicators.","risk":"CRITICAL: Shipping exact GPS coordinates without resolving this creates legal exposure (privacy regulations like GDPR in EU contexts treat worker location as sensitive personal data), driver attrition, and potential safety incidents. Shipping nothing leaves a competitive gap. The highest-risk path is choosing one side without executive alignment.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers (Recipients)","impact":"Want delivery visibility. Exact GPS is one solution but not the only one — ETA, progress stages, and proximity indicators also address the underlying need."},{"group":"Couriers (Drivers)","impact":"Directly affected by surveillance concerns. Exact GPS tracking visible to customers creates a power asymmetry and perceived (and real) loss of autonomy. Retention data suggests this is already causing attrition."},{"group":"Customer Success Team","impact":"Owns customer satisfaction metrics. Needs a tracking feature to reduce 'where is my order' support tickets and improve NPS."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Owns driver supply and retention. Cannot fulfill deliveries without drivers. Already experiencing measurable driver loss attributed to surveillance perception."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance","impact":"NOT YET CONSULTED but must be. Worker location data sharing with third parties (customers) may have regulatory implications depending on jurisdiction."},{"group":"Executive Leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate this conflict. The decision encodes a strategic priority: customer experience vs. driver experience. This is a CEO-level tradeoff, not an engineering decision."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Confidence is 40 because the specification contains a direct, unresolved contradiction between two authoritative stakeholder teams, both presenting their requirement as non-negotiable. No acceptance criteria can be defined until this is resolved. Additionally, legal/compliance has not weighed in, which could invalidate either position. The spec as written is unshippable.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and material — not a misunderstanding","Both stakeholder concerns are legitimate business needs","A middle-ground solution (abstracted location / ETA-based tracking) likely exists","Courier safety edge cases are hard constraints regardless of resolution","Driver retention impact is asymmetric and existential to the business"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which stakeholder priority should win — this is an executive decision","Regulatory requirements for courier location data in the target jurisdictions","Whether the driver retention problem is specifically caused by customer-visible tracking or by internal tracking tools","Quantified financial impact of each option"],"assumptions":["Both teams are accurately representing their stakeholder concerns","Driver attrition data attributed to surveillance is real and not confounded","No executive decision has already been made that one team is unaware of","The platform operates in jurisdictions where worker location privacy is relevant"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires exact GPS visibility to customers as a non-negotiable differentiator. Operations requires GPS to never be shown to customers as a non-negotiable retention measure. These are mutually exclusive requirements from peer-level teams. Engineering cannot resolve this — it is a strategic business priority decision.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Couriers","Customer Success","Operations","Executive Leadership","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["A: Full GPS transparency (Customer Success wins, Operations loses — risk: accelerated driver churn)","B: No location visibility (Operations wins, Customer Success loses — risk: competitive disadvantage, higher support load)","C: Abstracted tracking — show ETA, delivery stages, and proximity ('your courier is nearby') without exact coordinates. This satisfies the customer's underlying need (confidence) while respecting the courier's underlying need (dignity/safety). Neither team gets their literal requirement, but both get their actual business outcome.","D: Courier-consented tracking — let couriers opt in/out of location sharing, with incentives for opt-in. Respects autonomy but creates inconsistent customer experience."],"recommendation":"Option C (Abstracted Tracking) is the strongest domain-correct answer. In the logistics industry, the dominant pattern (UPS, FedEx, Amazon) is progress-based tracking ('out for delivery', '2 stops away', ETA) — not raw GPS. Exact GPS is not the industry standard for customer-facing tracking and introduces unnecessary privacy and safety risks. Customer Success's actual need is reducing uncertainty, not providing surveillance. Operations' actual need is protecting drivers, not hiding all progress. Abstracted tracking serves both underlying needs. However, this recommendation must be validated and approved by executive leadership — I cannot ship acceptance criteria against a contradicted spec."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Direct contradiction between two non-negotiable stakeholder requirements","business_rule_broken":"Cannot define business rules when the fundamental business intent is contested — any rule would encode one team's position over the other without authority to do so"},{"violation":"Legal/Compliance stakeholder not consulted","business_rule_broken":"Sharing worker GPS data with customers may violate privacy regulations (GDPR Article 88, local labor laws). No business rule can be finalized without legal review of courier location data sharing."}]}
